title: The Unbearable Urge to Crush What Is Too Beautiful

paragraphs: There is a particular emotional state, evidently widespread but lexically isolated in only a few languages, in which the perception of something overwhelmingly cute produces in the perceiver a sudden involuntary urge to bite, squeeze, or crush the object of perception. The state is most reliably triggered by infants — human and animal — and by small fluffy mammals more generally, though it can occur in the presence of any sufficiently overwhelming cuteness. The urge is universally felt to be inappropriate, is essentially never acted upon in a literal way, and is reported by approximately half of all surveyed adults across the cultures in which the research has been conducted. It has been studied by Yale psychologists under the clinical label 'cute aggression' since 2015. Filipino speakers had a word for it long before the psychologists arrived.

Gigil is the Tagalog noun and adjective for this state. The word covers a broader range than its English psychological gloss suggests: gigil can also describe the trembling intensity of barely-suppressed rage, or the physical tension of any overwhelming feeling that the body cannot quite contain. The cute-aggression sense is one of several, but it is the one for which the word has become most cited in international discussions of untranslatable emotion words. The Filipino assumption embedded in the word is that an overwhelming feeling — of love, of rage, of attraction, of revulsion — produces a recognisable physical state of contained tension that the body experiences as an urge to discharge through some kind of force, and that this state is the same state regardless of whether the underlying feeling is positive or negative.

What is interesting about the Yale research is what it has identified as the function of the urge. The proposed mechanism is that cute aggression is a regulatory response: the cuteness produces a positive affect so overwhelming that it threatens to incapacitate the perceiver, and the small surge of mock-aggression discharges enough of the affect to allow the perceiver to continue functioning. The 'I want to eat you up' said to a baby is, on this account, a small successful self-regulation that prevents the perceiver from becoming so overcome by the positive affect that they cannot, for example, change the baby's nappy or notice that the baby is hungry. The aggression is in the service of the care, not in tension with it.

If this is right — and the evidence so far is suggestive rather than conclusive — then gigil is one of a class of human emotional states that operate by means of internal contradiction. The same kind of structure appears in grief that produces a surge of physical energy, in fear that produces an urge to laugh, in tenderness that produces a desire to inflict mild pain. The body's emotional regulation is not a system that maintains each feeling at its appropriate level. It is a system that, when a feeling threatens to overwhelm the organism, generates a counter-feeling sufficient to restore functional capacity. The folk wisdom of phrases like 'I could just eat you up' or 'I love you so much it hurts' is older and more accurate than the analytic vocabulary has tended to credit.

What this suggests, more broadly, is that the emotional life of human beings is not well described by the model that treats feelings as discrete states each with its own appropriate intensity. The actual structure is closer to a homeostatic system in which overwhelming feeling of any kind triggers compensatory mechanisms — physical, behavioural, cognitive — that prevent the organism from being incapacitated. The mechanisms are visible most clearly in extreme cases (the inappropriate laughter at funerals, the rage that follows great loss, the cute aggression triggered by overwhelming tenderness) but they are operating continuously in the background of ordinary emotional life. The languages that have a word for gigil — or for one of the other small specific states in this family — have a finer-grained access to the homeostatic structure than the languages that lack the vocabulary.

What remains is the puzzle of why so many languages do not have such words. Cute aggression is, if the research is right, a near-universal human experience. Almost half of the adult population reports it. And yet most of the world's major languages handle it with awkward periphrasis or do not name it at all. The same is true for many of the small specific emotional states the literature has begun to investigate. The pattern suggests that lexical resources are not allocated by frequency of experience but by something else — perhaps by the relative importance of social discussion of the state, or by accidents of literary history, or by the deeper conventions of what a culture treats as a discussable feeling. The Filipino word has the experience. The languages that do not have the word have the experience too. The difference is in what becomes possible to think about, to share, to investigate, once the experience has been named.
essay.blockquote: Lexical resources are not allocated by frequency of experience — and the difference between having and not having the word is the difference between an experience that can be examined and one that cannot.

positions: Gigil and its small cousins in other languages name regulatory mechanisms of the human emotional system that the dominant Anglo-Saxon vocabulary has substantially failed to articulate. The languages that have these words give their speakers access to a more accurate phenomenology of emotional life, in which overwhelming feeling triggers compensatory counter-feeling rather than presenting itself as a pure stable state. The lack of equivalent vocabulary in English is a real impoverishment of self-understanding, not a neutral lexical accident.

The current vogue for collecting untranslatable emotion words risks treating each linguistic curiosity as evidence for a more profound psychological insight than the data actually support. Cute aggression is interesting; the cross-linguistic distribution of words for it is interesting; but the leap from lexical absence to phenomenological absence is exactly the kind of overgeneralisation that has plagued popular Sapir-Whorfian writing for a century. The careful version of the claim is much narrower than the popularised version usually implies.

number.label: Percentage of respondents in the 2018 Yale study of cute aggression who reported having experienced the urge to bite, pinch, or squeeze an infant — adult human, animal, or otherwise overwhelmingly cute object — within the previous month, across a sample drawn from multiple linguistic and cultural backgrounds.

fragment: The grandmother held the baby and made the noises one makes to babies. The baby's small face registered the sounds and responded with the small unguarded smile of a creature that does not yet know smiling is a transaction. The grandmother said to the baby, in Tagalog because the moment required it, that she could not stand how perfect the baby was, that she wanted to bite the baby's small toes, that she could not contain herself. The baby laughed without understanding. The grandmother kissed the baby's head three times with substantial force and then handed the baby back to its mother. She had managed the surge. She was now slightly out of breath. She had been waiting for this granddaughter for seventy years.

word.origin: Tagalog, the Austronesian language that forms the basis of Filipino, the national language of the Philippines. The word is in everyday use across Tagalog-speaking regions of the country and has been carried by speakers of Tagalog into Filipino diaspora communities worldwide. Its semantic range is broader than the cute-aggression sense for which it has become internationally cited: gigil can describe any state of barely-suppressed physical tension produced by an overwhelming feeling, whether of affection, rage, sexual attraction, or revulsion. The cute-aggression sense is the most-cited but not the only one.
word.definition: The state of overwhelming feeling — most often of affection or rage — that produces in the body a contained physical tension and an urge to discharge through some kind of force: a bite, a squeeze, a clenched fist, a sudden gesture. The state is felt as not entirely controllable and is typically discharged through small mock-aggressive actions that prevent the overwhelming feeling from incapacitating the person experiencing it.

quote.text: There is a certain love of suffering in love which is the most dangerous love of all — the love that loves so much it wants to bruise.
quote.author: James Baldwin

on_this_day.text: On 14 June 1898, in the small coastal town of Cavite Viejo south of Manila, the Philippine revolutionary leader Emilio Aguinaldo issued the formal proclamation of Philippine independence from Spanish colonial rule, in a declaration read aloud from the balcony of his family home as the new Philippine flag was unfurled for the first time in public. The independence so proclaimed would last less than a year before the United States, having defeated Spain in the war that began two months earlier, would assert its own colonial authority over the islands and initiate the Philippine-American War. The complexity of the moment — the genuine national emergence, the foreign manipulation, the brief reality of a sovereign republic almost immediately overwritten by a new empire — has shaped the way Tagalog speakers and the broader Philippine national community have understood their own twentieth-century history. The word gigil, as it happens, can also be used for the trembling barely-suppressed rage of a person watching a victory taken away from them in the hour of its arrival.

person.name: Lope K. Santos
paragraphs: Lope K. Santos was the Filipino writer, lexicographer, labour organiser, and grammarian whose 1906 grammar of Tagalog, the 'Balarila ng Wikang Pambansa,' was the first systematic native-authored description of the language and the basis on which Tagalog was officially adopted in 1937 as the foundation of what would become the Filipino national language. He was also the author of 'Banaag at Sikat' (Glimmer and Sunrise, 1906), one of the earliest novels written in Tagalog, a substantial social-realist work that introduced socialist political vocabulary into Filipino literature and that remains in print as a foundational text of the modern Tagalog tradition.

Santos's complication is that the standardisation of Tagalog into a national language has been politically contested across the entire subsequent history of the Philippines, in part because the country is home to dozens of substantial regional languages — Cebuano, Ilocano, Hiligaynon, Bikol, Waray, and others — whose speakers have not always welcomed the elevation of Tagalog to national status as either a linguistic or a cultural matter. The Filipino language as taught today in schools is the descendant of Santos's standardisation, and the work has been substantial and largely successful as a project of national integration, but the dispute about whether Tagalog should have been chosen — and about what would have been lost or gained by a different choice — has continued for a century and is unlikely to be settled. Santos worked through the early decades of that dispute and contributed substantially to its terms.

now_here.name: Banaue Rice Terraces
now_here.location: Ifugao Province, Cordillera Administrative Region, Philippines
now_here.text: A landscape of carved hillside rice terraces in the mountains of northern Luzon, built and maintained for approximately two thousand years by the Ifugao people, rising as high as 1,500 metres above sea level and constituting one of the most substantial pre-industrial earthworks in Southeast Asia. The terraces are not a museum: they are still farmed, still maintained, and still depend on the elaborate irrigation system the Ifugao have managed since the terraces were first cut into the hillsides. The maintenance has become more difficult as younger Ifugao have moved to lowland cities for work, and several sections of the terraces have begun to deteriorate as the labour-intensive repair work has become harder to sustain. The terraces are in this sense a slow-motion case study in what gigil — the sustained barely-suppressed intensity of caring for something — looks like as a multi-generational practice rather than as a moment, and in what happens when the cumulative weight of that practice exceeds what any single generation can carry.

questions.text: Have you experienced gigil in the cute-aggression sense — the urge to bite or squeeze in the face of overwhelming tenderness — and if so, how do you understand the urge in yourself?
questions.deeper: Do you think of the urge as a strange aberration, as a private secret, or as a recognisable feature of emotional life that simply happens to lack a word in your language? And what does the fact that other languages have a word for it change about how you regard the experience?
questions.experiment: The next time you notice the urge — toward a baby, an animal, a beautiful object — sit with it for a moment rather than discharging it. Notice the precise physical sensation, where it locates itself in the body, what it would feel like to release versus to contain. Notice also what you learn about the underlying tenderness by attending to the form its overflow takes.

questions.text: The essay suggests that overwhelming feelings of all kinds trigger compensatory counter-feelings as a homeostatic mechanism. Have you noticed this in your own emotional life — grief that produces inappropriate laughter, fear that produces calm, tenderness that produces mock-aggression?
questions.deeper: Do you treat the counter-feeling as a problem (a betrayal of the underlying feeling, a sign that you are not as moved as you should be) or as a feature (the system doing what it has to do to keep you functional)? And which reading produces the more honest relation to your own emotional life?
questions.experiment: If a strong emotional experience arrives this week, notice not only the named feeling but the small counter-currents it triggers in the body. Do not suppress them. Do not interpret them. Simply notice them as part of the larger emotional event, and observe what their presence reveals about how your emotional system actually works.

questions.text: Are there other small specific emotional states in your own experience that lack a name in your language — and that you suspect would be easier to live with, or to discuss, if a name were available?
questions.deeper: Could you name one yourself, even informally, in a way that would make it shareable with the people closest to you? And would the naming itself change the experience, or only its discussability?
questions.experiment: Pick one such state and try, this week, to describe it precisely to someone you trust. Notice both the difficulty of the description and what becomes possible in the relationship once a shared name — even a coined one — is available for the state.

trivia.question: The clinical research literature on the cute-aggression phenomenon that the Tagalog word 'gigil' has long named was substantially launched by a 2015 Yale study that proposed which functional explanation for the urge?
options: It is a vestigial predatory response, evidence of the evolutionary origins of human hunting behaviour
It is a regulatory mechanism that discharges overwhelming positive affect to prevent incapacitation
It is a learned cultural artefact specific to societies with neotenic features in their visual aesthetics
It is a sexual response misfiring in non-sexual contexts due to the dopamine reward system
trivia.explanation: The 2015 Yale study by Rebecca Dyer and Oriana Aragón proposed that cute aggression is a 'dimorphous expression of emotion' — a counter-feeling triggered when the underlying positive affect threatens to overwhelm the perceiver's capacity to function. The proposed mechanism is regulatory: the small surge of mock-aggression discharges enough of the overwhelming positive affect to allow the perceiver to continue with the practical care the situation usually demands (changing the baby, feeding the puppy, attending to the object of overwhelming feeling). Subsequent research has supported the regulatory interpretation, though the exact neural mechanisms remain incompletely understood.

data.definition: The soft, attentive form of affection that arises in response to the vulnerability or smallness of another being — the underlying emotional state from which cute aggression is, on the regulatory account, an overflow response. The feeling whose overwhelming intensity in the presence of an infant or a small animal generates, by a homeostatic mechanism, the urge the Tagalog word names.

movie.title: Norte, the End of History
movie.text: A loose Filipino reworking of Dostoevsky's 'Crime and Punishment,' set in the northern province of Ilocos Norte, in which a young law student murders a moneylender and an innocent man is convicted of the crime. The film runs for four hours and ten minutes, in characteristic Lav Diaz long takes, and devotes substantial screen time to the slow contained intensities of its three principal characters as they live through events that no single scene contains. Not a film about gigil in the cute-aggression sense; rather, a film about gigil in its broader Tagalog sense — the barely-suppressed physical tension of overwhelming feeling held in the body across hours, weeks, and years, in a culture whose vocabulary makes that holding nameable in ways the English-language tradition has historically struggled to articulate.
